original_abstract: While iron is an ideal candidate to replace precious metals in photoactive compounds, developing emissive and reactive iron complexes remains a significant challenge due to the incredibly rapid deactivation of their charge-transfer states. To date, emissive iron compounds are exceptionally rare, and dual emission has never been observed. In this work, we report the synthesis and characterization of a cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], which exhibits an unprecedented "Janus-type" dual emission originating from both ligand-to-metal (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) states. This unique photophysical profile is enabled by a specialized ligand framework that integrates four N-heterocyclic carbenes with two cyclometalating aryl units. The low-lying π* levels provided by the cyclometalating units create energetically accessible MLCT states that, crucially, do not deactivate into lower-energy LMCT pathways. With an exceptionally long lifetime of 4.6 ns, the MLCT-dominated state possesses potent reducing and oxidizing power capable of driving bimolecular electron transfer. These findings establish a new design strategy for iron-based luminophores and suggest a viable path for their application in sustainable photoredox catalysis.
humanized_abstract: Iron has emerged as a promising replacement metal for expensive transition metals in photoactive compounds. However, there remains a considerable challenge in designing iron(III) complexes with emissive properties and reactivity, owing to the high rate of deactivation of the charge-transfer (CT) state. Emissive iron(III) compounds are scarce and examples of dual emission have never been reported so far. Herein, we present a novel cyclometalated Fe(III) complex, [Fe(ImP)₂][PF₆], achieved via judicious design of a tridentate ligand bearing four N-heterocyclic carbenes (NHCs) with two cyclometalating aryl rings at the periphery, displaying “Janus-type” dual emission behavior through both ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer (MLCT) excited states. Moreover, the lifetime of the MLCT dominated state of this complex is as long as 4.6 ns endows with excellent reductive and oxidative ability, facilitating the bimolecular electron transfer process. Our work sheds new light on the rational design of iron-based luminophores, offering immense possibilities in the field of sustainable photoredox catalysis.
